title: The Time-Varying Price of Financial Intermediation in the Mortgage Market
abstract: We introduce a new measure of the price charged by financial intermediaries for connecting mortgage borrowers with capital market investors. Based on administrative lender pricing data, we document that the price of intermediation reacts strongly to variation in demand, reflecting capacity constraints of mortgage originators. This positive comovement of price with quantity reduced the pass-through of quantitative easing. We also find a notable upward trend in this price between 2008 and 2014, likely due to increased legal and regulatory burden in the mortgage market. The trend led to an implicit cost to borrowers of nearly $100 billion over this period.

article.introduction:
Mortgage lending is one ofthe main activities of the U.S. financial sector and a principal determinant of its growth in recent decades (Greenwood and Scharfstein,2013). For most U.S. mortgage transactions, intermediaries directly connect borrowers with capital market investors through the market for mortgage-backed securities (MBS), rather than funding loans from deposits or other sources.1One might expect the price of this service to be fairly low and stable. Instead, we show that the price of intermediation is highly volatile and exhibited a strong upward trend over the 2008 to 2014 period. We explore factors driving this variation and study its implications for the pass-through of monetary policy, specifically, the Federal Reserve's large-scale asset purchase programs commonly known as quantitative easing (QE).
  Our first contribution is to develop a new methodology to measure theprice of intermediationin the mortgage market, which we define as the payment made to the intermediary for linking the borrower with the ultimate supplier of funds for the loan. This payment covers costs associated with originating, underwriting, and servicing the loan and also may include a component representing profits. A way to think about the role of the intermediary is as a dealer that buys a loan (the right to future principal and interest payments) from the borrower and then sells it to investors in the form of an MBS. The first key input in our calculation is thus the value of a loan in the MBS market, which is what the intermediary receives. The second key input is the payment from the intermediary to the borrower; in addition to the loan principal, this payment includes a “rebate” that is a function of the interest rate on the loan.2These rebates are typically difficult to observe, but we are able to do so thanks to a unique administrative data set of “rate sheets” for conforming mortgages.
  We use our new methodology for measuring the price of intermediation in two different but complementary ways. First, we study the high-frequency (daily) pass-through of MBS price changes to rebates. We begin by showing that the pass-through of MBS price changes to the consumer mortgage market following QE announcements was immediate and strong overall, but that it varied substantially with the level of demand for mortgages (measured by daily applications for new loans) at the time of the announcement. More generally, at the daily frequency, rebates move very closely with prices in the MBS market. Pass-through, while high, is not symmetric: a one-dollar decrease in the MBS price translates into a one-dollar reduction in the rebate on the same day, while a one-dollar increase in the MBS price leads to only an 80 cent rebate increase. Importantly, high levels of demand substantially aggravate this asymmetry in pass-through—borrowers benefit less from MBS price improvements when application volume is already high.3
  Second, we study how the price of intermediation evolved at a lower (monthly) frequency. We document substantial variation over our sample period, with a cyclical component that is closely linked to the volume of mortgage demand and an upward trend that likely reflects increased costs of mortgage origination and servicing. Starting with the cyclical variation, we show that at a monthly frequency, a one-standard-deviation increase in application volume is associated with a 30 to 35 basis point (bp) increase in the price of intermediation (relative to an average of 142 bps). We use an instrumental variable (IV) strategy to show that the cyclical variation causally reflects changes in demand, exploiting the fact that changes in the gap between rates on outstanding mortgages and market interest rates (i.e., Treasury yields) should affect the demand for intermediation (by changing borrowers' incentive to refinance their loans) but not the supply, which should instead reflect the costs of underwriting and servicing mortgages. Our findings are consistent with the existence of significant capacity constraints in the mortgage industry, for which we also present direct evidence based on the processing time of new loan applications. These capacity constraints enable mortgage intermediaries to earn substantial profits during periods of high demand.
  We next turn to the upward trend. Using our baseline specification, we estimate that the price of intermediation rose 32 bps per year over our sample period. We use our data to test various potential explanations for this increase. We first isolate from the price of intermediation a component that reflects the net cost of servicing the loan after origination. Based on data on the valuation of mortgage servicing rights, we show that this component accounts for about half of the upward trend in the price of intermediation, likely reflecting higher direct costs of servicing driven by postcrisis regulations and the changed regulatory treatment of mortgage servicing rights. The remainder of the trend increase in price corresponds to intermediaries' markup (often referred to as “gain-on-sale”), and its upward trend could reflect an increase in either profits or origination-related costs. To measure origination costs, we turn to separate data from nonbank lenders reported to the Mortgage Bankers Association (MBA). We find that the growth of direct loan production expenses is roughly comparable to our estimate of the portion of price growth attributable to origination. The increase in costs was broad-based, though personnel costs account for more than two-thirds of the increase, and within this category “back end” costs increased the most, suggesting a need for more intense underwriting. In line with this view, the MBA data also reveal a downward trend in the number of monthly loan closings per employee. We thus conclude that even though profits of mortgage intermediaries reach high levels during periods of strong demand, there was no overall upward trend in their profits over this period.
  We estimate that over the 73-month period our sample covers, U.S. mortgage borrowers implicitly paid 155 billion dollars (in present value [PV] terms) to intermediaries for their services in originating and servicing mortgages, or about 25 billion per year on average. Without the cyclical variation and especially the upward trend, these costs would have been substantially lower. Specifically, the trend in the price of intermediation increased borrower spending on intermediation by $97 billion over this period, assuming no change in the time path of originations. Separate from the trend, our estimates imply that the sensitivity to cyclical variation in applications inflated expenditure on intermediation by another $11.6 billion.
  We use cross-sectional variation to test potential explanations for the patterns we document. First, we do not find evidence that intermediaries adjusted their pricing differentially for loan types or in locations with higher default risk. In fact, there is striking stability in the cross section over time: the price of intermediation went up for loans to borrowers with high credit scores as much as it did for loans with low credit scores, and the price went up as much in Miami, which was in the midst of a foreclosure wave, as it did in Salt Lake City, which was not. Thus, to the extent that intermediaries worried about factors such as “putback risk,” which is the risk that investors force them to buy back defaulted loans, this was reflected in more careful (and costly) underwriting across the board but not in differential prices. We also use city-level variation to test whether changes in local market concentration could have driven increased intermediation markups, but we find no evidence for this channel. This is also evident when simply looking at the time series of market concentration, which was falling throughout our sample while the price of intermediation rose.
  To the extent that the increases in the costs of mortgage origination and servicing reflect tighter regulation postcrisis, how should one interpret them? One view is that regulations generate deadweight losses as firms struggle to comply with complex rules. At the same time, one can also interpret the increased spending on underwriting and servicing mortgages as evidence of the effectiveness of the regulations. Proponents of regulations argue that lack of care in the origination process caused foreclosures and that lack of investment in servicing frustrated attempts to find beneficial resolutions for troubled loans. If higher costs across all loan types reflect broad-based increases in care in originating loans, we would expect broad-based declines in credit risk. Evidence from Goodman (2017) supports this mechanism: holding credit scores and loan-to-value (LTV) ratios constant, a Fannie Mae loan made between 2011 and 2015 is less than half as likely to default as a similar loan made between 1999 and 2003. On the servicing side, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau explicitly identified underinvestment in systems as a justification for requiring large cash payments to borrowers to settle accusations of servicing abuses.4Interpreting our evidence through this lens, an increase in the price of intermediation is evidence that policy changes had the desired effects, that is, is not an unintended consequence. Nevertheless, it is of course important to quantify the implied costs to borrowers, and to consider how more restrained credit intermediation interacts with monetary policy transmission.
  Our work relates to various strands of existing research. Most broadly, we contribute to the voluminous literature studying how monetary policy and financial market conditions affect the real economy through the credit channel (e.g., Bernanke et al.,1999; Bernanke and Gertler,1995; Kashyap and Stein,2000). Specifically, we focus on the link between financial markets (which may in turn be affected by monetary policy) and the borrowing rates faced by households; related contributions include Gertler and Karadi (2015) and Gilchrist et al. (2015). We zoom in on how the transmission from financial markets to borrower interest rates may be impaired by lender capacity constraints and cost increases.
  We also relate to work that studies the effects of QE on lending rates and volumes. Most directly, this paper builds on (and partly supersedes) Fuster and Willen (2010), who look at QE1 announcements in more detail based on an earlier version of the rate sheet data we use here. Hancock and Passmore (2011,2015) also study the impact of unconventional monetary policy on consumer mortgage rates, while Stroebel and Taylor (2012), Krishnamurthy and Vissing-Jorgensen (2011,2013), and Boyarchenko et al. (2019) focus primarily on MBS market spreads. The effects of QE announcements on the quantity of mortgage originations (especially refinancings) are studied by Fuster and Willen (2010), Beraja et al. (2019), and Di Maggio et al. (2019), who, respectively, focus on heterogeneity across borrower types, regions, and market segments.
  Our analysis of high-frequency pass-through from MBS prices to consumer prices relates to work in other consumer finance markets—see, for example, Ausubel (1991) and Calem et al. (2006) on the stickiness of credit card interest rates, Driscoll and Judson (2013) and Yankov (2014) on certificates of deposit, and Mora (2014) on auto loan rates. Mortgage pricing appears much more tightly linked to capital markets than other consumer interest rates; this could be due to the particular market structure that features brokers and loan officers (LOs) comparing offers across multiple intermediaries.5Hannan and Berger (1991), Neumark and Sharpe (1992), and Drechsler et al. (2017) link the slow adjustment of deposit rates to market power. In the mortgage market, Scharfstein and Sunderam (2013) study heterogeneity across counties in the sensitivity of rates and refinancing to changes in MBS yields, finding less pass-through in more concentrated markets. Buchak and Jørring (2021) argue that while concentration may have a small effect on rates, it has a larger effect on points and fees. In our sample period, market concentration decreased substantially, yet we find large increases in the price of intermediation. This is not to say that limits to competition cannot be an important source of profits; see, for instance, Agarwal et al. (2023), who document how competitive frictions enabled lenders to extract more surplus in the context of the Home Affordable Refinance Program (HARP).
  Related to our focus on capacity constraints in the mortgage market, Sharpe and Sherlund (2016) present evidence consistent with limited capacity affecting the types of loans that lenders choose to originate. Fuster et al. (2013), who focus on longer-term trends in intermediary margins, also present suggestive evidence of capacity constraints being a potentially important explanation of temporarily high margins.6More recently, Fuster et al. (2021) show that capacity constraints have remained a problem for the industry and drove up the price of intermediation over the large refinance wave during the COVID-19 pandemic. Other work focuses on the capacity constraints of mortgage servicers (who may be the same entity as the originators we study) and how these constraints have affected modification activity during the financial crisis (e.g., Cordell et al.,2009; Maturana,2017).
  Our argument that the upward trend in the price of intermediation partly reflects an increased legal and regulatory burden is consistent with other recent changes in the structure of the mortgage market. In particular, Buchak et al. (2018) study the determinants behind the rapid increase in market share of nondepository institutions (shadow banks) in mortgage originations, and find that this is due to an important extent to regulatory factors. Complementing our evidence on prices, D'Acunto and Rossi (2022) show a shift in the loan size distribution over the same period, from small and medium-sized loans to large (jumbo) loans, especially for the largest lenders. Similar to us, they conclude that increases in the fixed costs and per-loan costs of mortgage origination driven by regulatory changes postcrisis provide the best explanation of the empirical patterns.
  The paper is organized as follows. SectionIprovides a brief overview of intermediation in the mortgage market. SectionIIdescribes how we measure the different components of the price of intermediation. In SectionIII, we discuss event studies of different QE announcements, while SectionIVstudies high-frequency pass-through more broadly. SectionVstudies the evolution and determinants of the price of intermediation over time, and SectionVIfocuses on the aggregate implications of our findings. Finally, SectionVIIconcludes.
article.I. Financial Intermediation in the Mortg:
In this section, we provide a brief overview of intermediation in the mortgage market through the lens of a simple model. See, for example, Fuster et al. (2013) for a more extensive discussion of the institutional details.
  We study the market for financial intermediation in mortgages. We define financial intermediation as the service of matching a borrower with an MBS investor. Following industry convention, we refer to the consumer and MBS markets as the primary and secondary markets, respectively. We start with a lender/intermediary that makes a loan to a borrower with a fixed interest ratern$r^n$. A central feature of the U.S. mortgage market is that, in addition to providing the borrower with the principal on the loan, the intermediary pays a “rebate” to the borrower to cover closing costs and other expenses. This upfront payment, which goes by several different names (yield spread premium or YSP, service release premium, (negative) discount points), plays a central role in all mortgage transactions but is often not explicitly disclosed to the borrower, who simply sees the rebate in the form of changed closing costs (if the rebate is negative, the borrower is said to “pay points”).
  After the intermediary buys the loan from the borrower in the primary market, it sells it to investors in the secondary market. In general, intermediaries can sell loans to many different types of investors, but over the period we study, about 80% of new mortgage lending was funded through “agency” MBS guaranteed by the government-sponsored enterprises (GSEs) Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac or the government agency Ginnie Mae.7To facilitate discussion, we focus here on a loan securitized through Fannie Mae; the mechanics for Freddie Mac or Ginnie Mae are very similar.
  To sell loans in the secondary market, the intermediary assembles a pool of loans and then swaps the pool with Fannie Mae for an MBS collateralized by those loans. In addition to creating the MBS, Fannie Mae ensures timely payment of principal and interest of the security. To cover the cost, Fannie Mae collects a one-time upfront fee called the loan-level price adjustment (LLPA) from the issuer and ongoing monthly premiums called guarantee fees (g-fees) from the pool.
  The process of closing, packaging the loan into an MBS, and delivering it to an investor takes several weeks, but there is a highly liquid forward market—the to-be-announced or TBA market—for agency MBS. More or less immediately after agreeing to terms, that is, “locking” the loan, with the borrower, the intermediary can sell the loan forward in the TBA market. We definepTBAn$p_{TBA}^{n}$to be the price of a loan in the TBA market (per $100 principal) net of the anticipated payments to Fannie Mae associated with selling, securitizing, and insuring the loan. Since we study the market for intermediation, and not the market for mortgages per se, we takepTBAn$p_{TBA}^n$as exogenously given.
  Equation (2) implies the usual condition that the price equals the marginal cost, which in a perfectly competitive market with free entry would imply zero economic profit in equilibrium. We discuss the market structure further below.
  Figure1shows the equilibrium in the market for intermediation. The quantityq$q$on thex$x$-axis is the number of mortgages originated and the priceϕ$\phi$on they$y$-axis is the price of intermediation. If we assume increasing marginal costs, equation (2) implies that supply is upwardly sloping in quantity. Looking at the demand side, households demand fewer mortgages as the price of intermediation (ϕ$\phi$) goes up. To see why, note that holdingpTBAn$p_{TBA}^{n}$constant, any increase inϕ$\phi$reduces the rebate paid to the borrower for a given note ratern$r^n$.
  Figure1shows that the effect of a change inpTBA$p_{TBA}$onϕ$\phi$andq$q$and thus the level of pass-through depend on the shape of the supply curve. If the marginal cost of originating mortgages is increasing (as depicted), then an increase inpTBA$p_{TBA}$will lead to an increase inϕ$\phi$, and thus the pass-through to primary market prices (pYSP$p_{YSP}$) will be incomplete. If, instead, the marginal cost were constant, the supply curve would be flat and changes in TBA prices would completely pass through to rebates, meaning thatϕ$\phi$remains constant. In other words, more elastic supply implies more pass-through while incomplete pass-through does not imply imperfect competition.
  For simplicity, the discussion above considers a single note rate. In reality, intermediaries typically offer many different note rates,n=1,…,N$n=1,\ldots,N$, which differ in their secondary and primary market values. The first-order condition (2) still holds for alln$n$, and if the marginal costs of producing different note rates are equal,ϕn$\phi ^n$should be the same for alln$n$.
  It is important to note that the implicit price paid to the intermediary,ϕ$\phi$, is only one part of a borrower's total expenditure to get a mortgage. The borrower also faces expenses including appraisal, title insurance, an application fee, and a commission paid to the LO. Those expenses (which may be covered in part by the rebate) are beyond the scope of our analysis as these costs are not paid to the intermediary but rather to third parties.
  Mortgage intermediation is highly fragmented. According to data compiled pursuant to the Home Mortgage Disclosure Act (HMDA), there were 7,062 active mortgage lenders in 2014, down from 7,923 in 2010.8The top four firms in the industry accounted for 31% of origination volume in 2014 but the market share of the fifth-largest firm was only 3% and the tenth-largest less than 2%, indicating a large number of small but active lenders. Mortgage lenders typically operate in multiple states and a significant number operate nationally, meaning that local markets are also fragmented. For instance, according to HMDA data, more than 700 lenders were active in the Los Angeles metropolitan statistical area (MSA) each year over the 2008 to 2014 period, with a comparable share for the top four firms to their national share. See Amel et al. (2018) for additional discussion suggesting that the U.S. mortgage market is national in scope.
  Given these facts, the assumption of perfect competition made above does not seem unrealistic. However, it is important to stress that the assumption of perfect competition does not imply that we should not observe accounting profits in the mortgage market or that an increase in demand for intermediation will not increase returns for intermediaries. In particular, the marginal cost includes payments to all factors of production. If we think of intermediaries as owning capital, either physical like computer systems or nonphysical like reputational capital, then an increase in demand for intermediation will lead to increases in intermediaries' accounting profits. Similarly, it is well-known theoretically that when production capacity is fixed, Bertrand price competition can give rise to Cournot outcomes (Kreps and Scheinkman,1983).
  To the extent that there are temporarily high profits (or high rents for the factors of production) when demand is high, why does capacity not expand so that profits get competed away? A likely explanation is reluctance among lenders to incur costs to add additional capacity, given that refinancing-driven increases in volume are often relatively short-lived. Building a dynamic model of this market, featuring capacity adjustment costs and time-varying demand, represents an interesting direction for future work.
  What are the costs to the intermediary? Intermediaries play two functions in the life of the loan, namely, underwriting/originating the loan and then servicing the loan once it has been made.
  Origination costs can be divided into two parts: direct costs and risk management. Directs costs arise from the process of underwriting the loan. An intermediary must employ LOs to work with borrowers, underwriters to review applications, a compliance department to make sure that the LOs and underwriters are fulfilling their legal obligations, and so on. Additional costs include rent, IT expenses, and advertising.
  Risk management costs come from two main sources. The first, called “pipeline risk,” stems from the delay between the time a borrower locks a rate/rebate combination and the time the loan closes and is delivered to an MBS investor, typically somewhere between 30 and 90 days. While the originator enters into a forward contract to sell the loan to the investor, the borrower only enters into an option contract. Borrowers can and do decide not to exercise their option to close the loan for a variety of reasons ranging from property defects identified in home inspections to changes in interest rates that generate competing loan offers. The second major risk management challenge is credit risk. In theory, an originator offloads all credit risk when it delivers a loan to Fannie Mae, but in reality Fannie Mae reserves the right to “put back” the loan, that is, demand that the originator repurchase the loan if Fannie Mae discovers a defect in the loan. Putback risk was particularly salient in this period as Fannie Mae and other mortgage insurers used putbacks to impose enormous credit losses on originators during and after the Global Financial Crisis.9
  In addition to underwriting the loan, intermediaries' second main task is to service the loan, which consists of collecting monthly payments from the borrowers after the loan is made, and if the borrower becomes delinquent, working out a loss mitigation strategy or foreclosing. Servicers incur direct costs associated with these activities but servicing can also be a valuable source of short-term funding as there is a significant delay between the time when borrowers make payments to servicers and the time when those payments are due to investors. This “float” includes not only scheduled payments (principal and interest) but also unscheduled payments (loan payoffs due to sale or refinance) and thus can be quite substantial. The short-term funding role of float is particularly important for mortgage companies that use it to fund new originations. In addition, the servicer gains an opportunity to cross-sell financial products and has the inside track to refinance the loan. Thus, the net cost of servicing could be positive or negative.
  One important institutional detail in agency MBS relates to how the servicer gets compensated. For as long as a loan is open, a servicer receives a monthly payment equal to 25 bps (annual) of the mortgage amount. As discussed above, the servicer receives additional income (or potential benefits), but also incurs costs. Our baseline calculation ofϕ$\phi$does not separately measure these additional costs/benefits to the servicer, and implicitly values the 25 bps of servicing based on the value of the cash flow in the MBS market.
article.II. Measurement:
In this section, we describe our approach to calculating the components of the price of intermediationϕ$\phi$, defined in equation (1). We first discuss how to measurepYSPn$p_{YSP}^{n}$, including how we choosern$r^n$, the note rate of the mortgage. We then turn to the question of how to measurepTBAn$p^{n}_{TBA}$, the price of a given mortgage in the secondary market.
  Intermediaries publish daily rate sheets, which in effect are a menu of combinations of note rates and rebates. Figure2provides an example. The upper-left panel shows that if the borrower commits to close a 30-year fixed-rate mortgage within 30 days with a note rate of 4.625%, the lender will pay the borrower a 70 cent rebate per 100 dollars of principal. As mentioned already, rebates can be negative. If instead the borrower chooses a 4.25% rate, the rebate would be −130 cents per 100 dollars of principal, that is, the borrower would pay the lender.
  Our rate sheet data come from a company called Optimal Blue (formerly known as LoanSifter), which provides data on rates and rebates to LOs in the third-party origination (TPO) segment of the market. TPO refers to the fact that the originator is a broker or, as is the case for most LOs in Optimal Blue, works for a nonbank “correspondent” firm that funds and originates loans that are then sold to other financial institutions (e.g., large banks). Over the period of our study, TPO accounted for about one-half of the U.S. mortgage market.13
  Our sample starts on October 16, 2008 and ends on October 31, 2014, which, after removing missing and inconsistent observations, yields 1,523 days of data. Optimal Blue provides LOs with a search engine that allows the LO to enter loan characteristics such as the LTV ratio, the borrower's FICO score, and the loan amount and to see different intermediaries' offers for such a loan. Importantly, these offers are binding on the intermediary. If the LO selects an offer and delivers a loan that satisfies the underwriting guidelines, the intermediary has an obligation to fund it at the chosen rate/rebate combination. Intermediaries typically issue the first rate sheet of the day around 10 a.m. and then revise it frequently over the course of the day.14Our data include only a single snapshot taken at the end of the day, meaning we have the last rate sheet issued on any given day.15
  Our main data set consists of 343,133 lender×$\times$day×$\times$rate observations. This data set is substantially smaller than the raw data, which report all offers made to all LOs by all intermediaries (lenders) with whom the LO has a relationship, that is, all LO×$\times$lender×$\times$day×$\times$rate observations. In some cases, a lender will make different offers for the same loan depending on the identity of the LO.16Thus, to collapse to the lender level, we average the offers for a given lender, date, and rate across LOs. In some rare cases, a single LO will receive two different offers for the same rate from the same lender, in which case we discard the inferior offer. Rates are almost always in 1/8 point increments, but on those rare occasions that they are not, for example, 4.99 versus 5, we round to the nearest 1/8.
  Further information about the Optimal Blue data set can be found in TableI. The number of lenders over which Optimal Blue searches fluctuates in our sample. Part of this variation reflects the entry and exit of lenders from the correspondent segment; for example, Bank of America exited in 2011.17In addition, any change in the set of lenders with whom a specific LO has a relationship may lead to a change in the number of offers in our sample. We do not know the identities of the individual lenders, but do know that essentially all of the largest lenders are in the Optimal Blue data.
  In much of our analysis, we focus on pricing for a “plain-vanilla” 30-year fixed-rate mortgage on an owner-occupied property located in Los Angeles, CA, with a loan amount of $300,000, an LTV ratio of 80%, and a borrower with a FICO of 750 (see Panel A of TableI). As discussed later, we consider alternative scenarios as well.18
  Panel B of TableIshows basic features of our data set. On the median day, we see 21 different lenders, and each lender offers a menu of 10 rate-rebate combinations. Lenders typically offer rates in 1/8 point increments, so 10 rates imply a within-lender range of about 1.125%. Variation in the range of rates is relatively small with a range of 1% for almost half of all lender-days. However, while the range of rates does not vary much, the range of rebates offered does, with within-lender ranges of less than 4 percentage points at one end to almost 7 percentage points at the other.
  One key insight from Panel B of TableIis that the trade-off between rates and rebates is not constant. In other words, the effect of a given rebate on the rate will change over time. Suppose our baseline borrower with a $300,000 loan needs a $3,000 or 1% rebate to cover closing costs. Panel B shows that 10% of the time the borrower will need to absorb at most a1%/6.02=17$1\% / 6.02 = 17$bp increase in rates, and 10% of the time, the same borrower will have to pay at least1%/3.00=33$1\% / 3.00 = 33$bps more.
  In principle, we could choose a single note rate and follow that note rate over our entire sample period. This is not possible, however, since the set of note rates quoted changes often as market interest rates evolve over time.19To address this issue, we construct a constant-rebate note rate that we call “Rate101.” Specifically, Rate101 is the note rate corresponding to a rebate of +1 point (orpYSP=101$p_{YSP}= 101$), which is anecdotally a typical rebate received by borrowers. We construct Rate101 for each rate sheet by interpolating between different offers and then use the daily median value across our sample of rate sheets as our baseline. The interpolation is required because, as Figure2shows, rebates are quoted in decimals but note rates are generally quoted in 1/8 of a percentage point. In the rare case in which the lender only offers rebates above or below 101, we exclude the lender from our sample for that day.
  The top panel of Figure3shows the evolution of Rate101 over our sample period (taking the median across lenders at a given point in time). Rate101 started above 6% in 2008, prior to the monetary policy actions that started in late November of that year, and reached its low point near the end of 2012 at close to 3%. The bottom panel shows how Rate101 compares to two alternative choices, Rate100 (for a rebate of zero) and the widely quoted Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Market Survey (PMMS) rate.20The patterns in the figure are instructive. For most of our sample, from 2010 to 2014, the Freddie Mac Rate is, on average, very close to Rate101, validating our claim that a one-point rebate is standard. However, the figure also shows that the gap between Rate101 and Rate100 was high and volatile in 2009; at some points in that year, a borrower needed to pay an 80 bp higher rate to receive a 100 bp rebate.21Not surprisingly, in 2009, borrowers were rebate-averse and the Freddie Mac rate was closer to Rate100 than Rate101. As a result, where relevant, we use Rate100 rather than Rate101 (see Figure4, for example). After 2009, the gap between Rate101 and Rate100 stabilized.
  The main secondary market for mortgages is called the to-be-announced, or TBA, market. The TBA market is a forward market for MBS guaranteed by Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, and Ginnie Mae, and is one of the largest fixed-income markets in the world (Vickery and Wright,2013). In this market, buyers (such as mutual funds, pension funds, foreign investors, and, in recent years, the Federal Reserve) promise to buy a pool of mortgages meeting certain specified characteristics at a specific date in the future. The majority of newly produced agency MBS pools are traded in this market. Importantly, since the characteristics of the loan pool that will be delivered are not known at the time of the transaction, pricing does not differ with individual loan characteristics that could affect prepayment risk, and instead reflects the typical characteristics that would be delivered. This also means that for some loan types with particularly attractive prepayment characteristics, selling in the TBA market may be dominated by selling in the less liquid “specified pool” market segment at a higher price (“pay-up”). For our baseline loan, which does not feature any characteristics that would typically receive a pay-up, the assumption that such a loan would be sold in the TBA market seems uncontroversial.22
  As mentioned earlier, the guaranteeing agency, in our case Fannie Mae, charges an ongoing insurance premium or g-fee as well as an upfront premium depending on loan characteristics, known as LLPA. The exact values of g-fees are not disclosed, but we know that they vary over our sample. Up to 2012, the typical g-fee was roughly 22 bps, meaning that Fannie charged an annualized fee of 0.22% of the unpaid principal balance of a loan every month.23In 2012, FHFA, Fannie and Freddie's regulator, announced two 10 bp increases. The first, on January 1, raised the g-fee to 32 bps and the second, on September 1, raised it to 42 bps, where it remained for the rest of our sample. The upfront LLPA for the base-case loan characteristics changes only once over our sample period, from 25 bps prior to December 23, 2010 to 50 bps.24
  The MBS price data are from J.P. Morgan and, for simplicity, we assume that all loans are sold to Fannie Mae. For our baseline analysis, we normalize all prices to be 45 days to settlement using a weighted combination of the one-, two-, and three-month-out contracts.25This is the relevant metric if the time between rate lock (prior to loan origination) and delivery in a TBA trade is 45 days, which is close to the approximate average lag from application to origination. Different normalizations do not materially affect our results since the gaps between the prices are generally stable.
  To measure time-series variation in demand and loan-processing times, we use new loan applications from the confidential version of the HMDA data set, which captures a large share (roughly 90%) of mortgage applications and originations. The confidential version of the data contains the month and date of application and action (e.g., origination or rejection), which allows us to count applications at a daily level and to measure the number of days between application and action. We include all first-lien, single-family loans in our measure, including applications for refinancing and purchase loans. In our lower-frequency analysis in SectionV, we use total monthly applications normalized by the number of business days in a given month.
  We also use the HMDA data to measure lender concentration, by calculating the market share of the top four lenders (following Scharfstein and Sunderam,2013) at a monthly frequency. This measure is specific to the MSA in question, which is Los Angeles in our baseline case (since we are looking at rate/rebate offers for Los Angeles); in SectionV.B.3we study the effects of local concentration across 40 MSAs. The overall trends in market concentration are similar across the country during the period we study.
  As a measure of labor costs, we use real estate credit payrolls divided by real estate credit employment. Both series are compiled by the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) and available at a monthly frequency. To measure the volatility of interest rates, which may affect originators' hedging costs, we use the monthly average Merrill Lynch Option Volatility Expectations (MOVE) index, an index of the normalized implied volatility on one-month Treasury options, weighted on the 2-, 5-, 10-, and 30-year contracts.
  Finally, as discussed in SectionI.D, for our alternative measure of intermediary marginsπ$\pi$, we use servicing valuation multiples provided to us by MIAC. We have monthly data from the start of our data in October 2008 until September 2014; we assume that the value in October 2014 is the same as in September 2014.26For the valuation of the remaining interest strip (after g-fee and 25 bp servicing are deducted from the coupon), we interpolate between the TBA prices of the surrounding coupons, as we do in our baseline calculation.
article.III. Event Studies: QE Announcements:
To explore the evolution of prices in the primary and secondary mortgage markets, we start with Rate101 for datet¯$\overline{t}$, which we take to be the day before a given announcement. Denote bypYSPRate101,s(t)$p^{Rate101,s}_{YSP}(t)$the time-t$t$median primary market price of a loan with a note rate of time-s$s$Rate101, whilepTBARate101,s(t)$p^{Rate101,s}_{TBA}(t)$similarly denotes the secondary market price. We then consider different datest$t$and calculatepYSPRate101,t¯(t)$p^{Rate101,\overline{t}}_{YSP}(t)$andpTBARate101,t¯(t)$p^{Rate101,\overline{t}}_{TBA}(t)$.
  To illustrate this, consider the announcement of QE3 in September 2012 (the bottom left panel of Figure4). TableIIshows how we compute the two series for the date immediately prior to the announcement on September 12 and the date immediately following, September 13, illustrating that we keep the note rate constant over time and allow the primary and secondary market prices to move. The two series imply that 55% (0.6/1.1$0.6/1.1$) of the secondary market price increase was passed through to borrowers on the day of the QE3 announcement. The figure runs the same calculation for subsequent dates and shows thatpTBA$p_{TBA}$drifted down over the next few days. Meanwhile,pYSP$p_{YSP}$stayed more or less the same, so the effective pass-through was somewhat higher at longer horizons.
  Looking across the six events in Figure4, several features are worth noting.28First, the high-frequency relationship betweenpTBA$p_{TBA}$andpYSP$p_{YSP}$is quite close. Almost everywhere, changes in the secondary market price lead to changes in the primary market price on the same day. It is important to stress that there is nothing mechanical about this relationship in the data as the two time series come from completely different data sources:pTBA$p_{TBA}$is generated from global financial markets data andpYSP$p_{YSP}$comes from lender offers to LOs working with individual borrowers.
  The second notable feature of Figure4is that the pass-through appears to vary significantly across the different events. For example, borrowers received less than 50% of the effects of the QE1 Expansion announcement in March 2009. In contrast, the effects of the initial announcement of QE1 in November 2008, the Taper Tantrum in June 2013, and the Nontaper in September 2013 were passed on to borrowers almost entirely. What factors can explain these differences? In SectionI.Aabove, we argued that the degree of pass-through depends on the shape of the supply curve. Recall that, all else equal, an increase in prices in the secondary market leads to an increase in the demand for intermediation, and that a higher slope of the cost curve implies lower pass-through, as depicted in Figure1.
  To explore the effect of demand on pass-through, we add the daily volume of new loan applications to each figure. The relationship between application volume and pass-through across events suggests that intermediaries face convex costs in quantity. On November 24, 2008, applications were at historically low levels and pass-through was close to complete. In contrast, the day before the announcement of the QE1 Expansion, applications had nearly doubled relative to November 24 and pass-through was around 50%.29
  A third feature of Figure4is that the price of intermediationϕ$\phi$appears to rise over time. If we compare the original announcement of QE1 (top left) with the Nontaper (bottom right), the pass-through was stronger in the latter, but the gap between intermediaries' revenue (pTBA$p_{TBA}$) and what they pay the borrower for the loan (pYSP$p_{YSP}$) is much higher in the latter. This increase inϕ$\phi$could, according to the theory, result from higher volume, but the data appear to reject that possibility—application volume in September 2013 was very similar to application volume before the QE1 announcement, butϕ$\phi$was much higher.
  In the next two sections, we consider questions raised here using an econometric approach. In particular: How strong is pass-through? Does volume systematically affect pass-through? Is pass-through higher for decreases in prices versus increases? Did the price of intermediation increase over time? What accounts for that increase?
article.IV. High-Frequency Pass-Through:
We now study the extent to which changes in MBS prices from one day to the next are reflected in the rebates offered on rate sheets. To do so, we construct a daily time series forΔpYSPRate101,t(t)$\Delta p^{Rate101,t}_{YSP}(t)$by computing Rate101 and the change inpYSPRate101$p^{Rate101}_{YSP}$at the lender-day level, taking the median by day, and then regressing that onΔpTBARate101,t(t)$\Delta p^{Rate101,t}_{TBA}(t)$, which we compute using the median daily Rate101 across lenders. Figure5provides a scatterplot of the two sets of changes. As can be seen, there is a very strong relationship between price changes in the primary and secondary markets, although the pass-through appears slightly stronger for negative changes than positive changes.
  TableIIIreports results from a linear regression performed to quantify the amount of pass-through in Figure5. Column (1) shows that the average pass-through coefficient is 0.92, and that 88% of the variation in the rebate changes is explained by the variation in MBS price changes. Moreover, the pass-through of price decreases is stronger than the pass-through of price increases (column (2)). The asymmetry of pass-through is statistically and economically significant, with approximately 100% of price decreases being passed through within this one-day window. In contrast, only 80% of price increases are passed through on the first day. In column (3), we add the price change from the previous day and find that there is some additional pass-through of lagged positive (but not negative) changes, so that over two days, approximately 87% of MBS price increases are reflected in primary market rebates. (We experimented with adding further lagged MBS price changes but found their effects to be statistically indistinguishable from zero.)
  In the remaining two columns, we explore a potential factor driving time variation in pass-through, namely, the level of demand. Specifically, we interact the MBS price changes with the standardized level of new applications from HMDA (lagged by one day), so that the coefficient on the interaction term corresponds to the effect on daily pass-through of a one-standard-deviation increase in application volume. Column (4) shows that a higher level of applications does indeed reduce the pass-through of MBS price increases to rebates. For instance, the coefficients imply that if applications are two standard deviations above average, only 59% (=0.787−2∗0.097$=0.787-2*0.097$) of a price increase is passed through. The final column shows that this interaction effect remains unchanged if we add the lagged price change as well. The uninteracted lagged application level is not significantly related to changes inpYSP$p_{YSP}$.30
  While this analysis focuses on the pass-through of prices, it is of course equally important to ask how MBS price changes affect loan quantities. InInternet Appendix31TableIA.I, we show that there is a strong positive relationship between MBS price changes and the number of daily applications (and the number of originated loans stemming from these applications): a one-dollar change inpTBA$p_{TBA}$is on average associated with about a 7% change in applications and a 9% change in originations on the same day. The price changes have a further positive effect of similar size the following day; for originations, the combined effect amounts to a 17% change per $1 change inpTBA$p_{TBA}$. As one would expect, these effects are much stronger for refinance quantities than for home purchases, given that the former are generally directly motivated by the goal of obtaining a lower mortgage rate.32
  The takeaway from these regressions is that at high frequencies, prices and quantities in the primary mortgage market respond strongly to the MBS market. In a sense, this should not be surprising, given that lenders issue new rate sheets every day and sometimes more than once a day. But this result does contrast with findings of infrequent price adjustments in other consumer finance markets, where rates offered to consumers react only slowly to changes in monetary policy and market rates (e.g., Ausubel,1991; Driscoll and Judson,2013). One possible explanation for the difference is that brokers and correspondent lenders play the role of delegated comparison shoppers for mortgage borrowers and make the market more competitive than other consumer finance markets. In their study of the Canadian mortgage market, Allen et al. (2014) argue that brokers play such a role there.
  Although these high-frequency regressions demonstrate a direct and strong link from MBS prices to the primary market, the result that increases in MBS prices result in incomplete pass-through, especially when the level of loan demand is high, leaves open the possibility of lower-frequency movements in the price of intermediation, which we turn to next.
article.V. Evolution of the Price of Intermediat:
We estimateϕi(t)$\phi _i(t)$for each lender-day and then take monthly averages within-lender to get a panel with 1,670 lender-month observations.
  Panel A of Figure6shows the cross-lender mean price of intermediationϕ$\phi$in our sample.33Two main observations stand out. The first is that there was a strong upward trend in the price over our sample. As illustrated by the linear time trend in the figure,ϕ$\phi$on average grew by 27 bps per year, corresponding to a total increase of 1.62 percentage points over our sample period. The second observation is thatϕ$\phi$exhibits large fluctuations around the trend. Panel B of the figure shows that detrendedϕ$\phi$is very highly correlated (ρ=0.78$\rho =0.78$) with monthly mortgage application volume. Panel A further displays the evolution of our gain-on-sale measureπ$\pi$, which displays similar cyclicality but a less pronounced upward trend.
  The general patterns displayed in our series, large high-frequency changes and an upward trend over time, are consistent with other measures of the price of intermediation. FigureIA.2in theInternet Appendix, for example, shows a high correlation (ρ=0.74$\rho =0.74$) betweenπ$\pi$and an alternative measure based on mortgage gain-on-sale margins reported by large banks. Moreover, FigureIA.6shows a longer time series of an approximatedϕ$\phi$based on the Freddie Mac PMMS rate and indicates that the level ofϕ$\phi$in late 2008 was not unusually low relative to previous years.
  The remainder of this section proceeds as follows. In SectionV.A, we focus on cyclical variation inϕ$\phi$(andπ$\pi$) and show the central role of mortgage applications. In SectionV.B, we consider the upward trend inϕ$\phi$.
  Columns (1) and (2) of TableIV, Panel A, present a regression version of the results in Figure6. In contrast to the figure, whereϕ$\phi$is averaged across lenders, the dependent variable in the regression is measured at the lender-by-month level, which means we are able to directly account for changes in sample composition over time via lender fixed effects. Column (2) shows that a combination of a linear time trend and application volume can explain about 64% of the within-lender variation inϕ$\phi$. The point estimate implies that a one-standard-deviation increase in average daily applications, or about 13,000 applications, leads to a 33 bp increase in the price of intermediation. Holding applications constant,ϕ$\phi$grew 2.7 bps per month (or about 32 bps a year) over our sample.
  Our findings on the relationship between the price of intermediation and applications are robust to many different specifications. In column (3), we add other potentially cyclical variables (normalized to have a mean of zero and a standard deviation of one). Of those, real estate payroll (defined as the dollar payroll per employee in the real estate credit sector, obtained from the BLS) has a significant effect onϕ$\phi$, but mostly at the expense of the time trend, not the effect of applications, as we discuss in SectionV.B.34
  Since we use a measure of the price of intermediation as the dependent variable, and applications (quantity) on the right-hand side, one concern is that the coefficient on applications is biased or the correlation is spurious (since quantity is of course endogenous to price). The first thing to keep in mind is that a higher price should correlate with a reduced quantity (holding everything else fixed), so this effect should bias our coefficient of interest downward or even lead to a negative coefficient, yet we find a stronglypositivecoefficient. Thus, this endogeneity concern works against our main finding.
  However, to further assess how important this bias could be, we also employ an IV strategy. As our instrument, we need something that affects the application volume without directly affecting the price of intermediation. A proxy for refinancing demand is most promising, since time-series variation in mortgage applications, especially at relatively high frequency, is driven primarily by variation in refinancing. We use the difference between the WAC on outstanding conventional MBS and the 10-year constant-maturity Treasury yield. This measure is closely correlated with borrowers' rate incentive to refinance (which would be given by the WAC minus the current mortgage rates) but is not affected by current developments in the mortgage market itself. In terms of the supply/demand relationship shown in Figure1, one can think of an increase in (WAC − Treasury yield) as proxying for an upward shift of the demand curve, which allows us to trace out the effect on the equilibrium price.35
  Panel A of Figure7shows the evolution of the WAC on outstanding conventional mortgages and the 10-year Treasury yield. Not surprisingly, the latter is responsible for most of the high-frequency variation in the difference between the two series. Panel B shows that this difference is closely correlated with application volume over our sample period, both in levels (ρ=0.87$\rho =0.87$) and in monthly changes (ρ=0.73$\rho =0.73$).
  The IV versions of the regressions without and with additional controls are shown in columns (4) and (5) of TableIV, Panel A. The coefficient on applications increases slightly compared to the OLS versions, as one would expect if higher intermediation prices tend to lead to fewer applications, other things remaining equal. The first-stageF$F$-statistics, shown in the last row of the table, indicate a strong explanatory power of our excluded instrument for applications, the potentially endogenous variable.
  When we use gain-on-saleπ$\pi$as the dependent variable in columns (6) and (7), the time trend is affected but the relationship with applications remains strongly positive and highly significant. Also of note, higher interest rate volatility, as measured by the Merrill Lynch MOVE index, is significantly positively correlated withπ$\pi$. This makes sense, since higher volatility increases hedging costs and intermediaries require compensation for this through higher gain-on-sale. The final two columns with(ϕ−π)$(\phi -\pi)$as the dependent variable are discussed below.
  In Panel B of TableIV, we rerun the same regressions in monthly changes and continue to find strong effects of applications onϕ$\phi$andπ$\pi$. Focusing on the IV specifications in columns (3) to (6), the coefficients are slightly smaller than in the corresponding regressions in levels in Panel A, but a one-standard-deviation increase in applications still increasesϕ$\phi$andπ$\pi$by 26 to 37 bps. Of the other control variables, only interest rate volatility continues to have a significant positive effect (onπ$\pi$).
  We note that these regressions focus on estimating the effects of contemporaneous (changes in) application volumes on pricing. However, there may also be dynamic and persistent effects. On the one hand, if application volumes grow for several months in a row, that will lead to a high number of applications “in progress” at lenders, which in turn may tighten capacity constraints and lead to larger effects on pricing of an additional increase in applications. On the other hand, to the extent that lenders expect high application volumes to persist, that may lead them to expand their workforce, which in turn may ease capacity constraints and reduceϕ$\phi$. As Figure6illustrates, our data only contain three (extended) periods of high demand, so empirically disentangling the two channels above is challenging. Examining the pattern over 2012 to 2013 does provide some suggestive evidence in favor of the second channel, asϕ$\phi$eventually started to fall in late 2012 and early 2013 even as application volumes remained high. Fuster et al. (2021) show that between January 2012 and April 2013, U.S.-wide employment of mortgage LOs according to the BLS grew by about 18%, from 250,000 to 294,000.
  In sum, we find a strong and plausibly causal effect of mortgage application volumes on the price of intermediation. Applications proxy for increasing marginal costs, or increased pricing power due to limited origination capacity, as discussed earlier. We next present direct evidence on capacity constraints becoming more binding during periods of high demand.
  We view the strong positive correlation betweenϕ$\phi$and application volume documented above as indicative of limited capacity in mortgage intermediation. To further support our interpretation, we present evidence based on the processing times for mortgage applications measured in the HMDA data. Figure8shows, at the monthly frequency, the number of new mortgage applications plotted against the median processing time of applications submitted that month (i.e., the number of days between the application date and the “action” date, when either the loan closes or the application is denied or withdrawn). The figure shows that when the number of new mortgage applications increases, it takes longer to process additional applications. The correlation between the two series in levels is 0.74; in changes it is 0.63. The variation in processing times can be substantial; for instance, it goes from 25 days to about 40 days following the November 2008 Fed announcement. This evidence suggests that the capacity of mortgage originators to process applications is limited.
  A regression analysis, shown in TableV, confirms the significance of this relationship. With OLS, we find that a one-standard-deviation increase in monthly applications is associated with an increase in processing time of 3 to 3.5 days. This result is robust to geographic and lender fixed effects as well as granular loan-level controls, and holds if we look at originated loans only or if we include all application outcomes. When we use the same IV strategy as above, using (WAC − 10-year Treasury yield) as the instrument for applications, the effect size increases to about 4.7 days per one-standard-deviation increase in applications, indicating a strong causal link from mortgage demand to processing times.36
  The table also shows that conditional on application volume, there was a significant upward trend in processing time over this period, amounting to about 2 to 2.5 days per year for originated loans over our sample period. This finding is consistent with industry evidence of decreased origination productivity since 2008, as we discuss below.
  Aside from the cyclical variation, the upward trend in the price of intermediation in our sample is striking—in our baseline specification, the price rises by 32 bps a year, corresponding to $800 for a $250,000 loan. What drives this increase? In this section, we study the role and contribution of increased profits and increased costs in the documented upward trend ofϕ$\phi$. We do so first by studying our time-series evidence. We then turn to cross-MSA regressions. Finally, we examine direct evidence on increased costs based on lender data collected by the MBA.
  Our regressions in TableIVshed light on some of the possible explanations for the upward trend in the price of intermediation. As noted above, our measure of payroll per employee in the real estate credit sector is strongly positively related toϕ$\phi$andπ$\pi$(at least in levels), and including it in the regression reduces the estimated time trend. This suggests that an increase in the labor cost involved in the origination process could be an important part of the upward trend.
  In contrast, the regressions provide less support for the hypothesis that lenders were able to extract higher prices due to increased market power: there is no positive relation between market concentration andϕ$\phi$orπ$\pi$over this period, which is not surprising given that concentration trended down over this time (so regressingϕ$\phi$orπ$\pi$on concentration alone actually yields a negative and significant coefficient).37We further examine the role of market concentration in our cross-MSA analysis below. Finally, interest rate volatility, although positively related to gain-on-saleπ$\pi$, has little effect on the upward trend, since it trended downward over the sample period (see FigureIA.3).
  As discussed in SectionI.D, the gap (ϕ−π$\phi -\pi$) can be interpreted as the PV of servicing costs. The evolution of this gap can be derived from the two lines in Panel A of Figure6. Early in our sample period,π$\pi$is larger thanϕ$\phi$, meaning that the MIAC servicing multiples were higher than the MBS-price-implied multiples. This implies that the additional benefits from servicing exceeded the costs. Anecdotally, this was typical in the precrisis period more generally. However, in late 2009 the relationship betweenπ$\pi$andϕ$\phi$flipped, meaning that the additional costs of servicing exceeded the benefits. This implies that the PV of net servicing costs increased over our sample period.
  The final two columns of TableIVshow that the estimated upward trend in these costs accounts for about half the total upward trend inϕ$\phi$: we estimate that on average, net servicing costs increased by 1.3 to 1.5 bps per month over this period.
  It is also worth discussing the other coefficients. The PV of servicing costs displays a significantly negative correlation with applications (especially in monthly changes). This finding is likely driven by the fact that mortgages originated at a time of high application volume tend to have a shorter expected lifespan (precisely becauseϕ$\phi$, or alternatively, the gap between the mortgage note rate and MBS yields, is high at those times and expected to decrease in the future). Similarly, high interest rate volatility decreases the expected lifespan of a mortgage (since borrowers' prepayment option becomes more likely to be in-the-money going forward) and thus the expected PV of servicing costs decreases. InInternet Appendix Section II, we show that (ϕ−π$\phi -\pi$) is indeed significantly positively correlated with direct model-based proxies for the expected lifespan of new mortgages.
  In sum, our analysis suggests that net servicing costs increased over 2008 to 2014. This result is consistent with evidence of an increase in the direct costs of servicing over this period (see FigureIA.4) and also with the increased regulatory cost for banks of holding mortgage servicing rights under Basel III (see Hendricks et al.2016). Consistent with the latter channel, a significant shift in servicing activity from banks to nonbanks occurred after the financial crisis. According to Kaul and Goodman (2016), the share of single-family mortgages serviced by nonbanks increased from 6% in 2010 to 31% in 2015.
  As discussed in SectionI.C, lenders who sell loans to Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac face residual credit risk because the GSEs can force lenders to repurchase defaulted loans if some flaw in the underwriting or documentation is found. As the GSEs used this option aggressively in the wake of the financial crisis, this form of credit risk, known in the industry as putback risk, became commonly cited as a reason behind tighter lending standards (e.g., Federal Reserve Board,2013). Thus, from the perspective of the loan originator, the credit risk of the loan may have become a more salient concern and they may have wanted to earn a larger premium for bearing and reserving against this risk.
  If increased credit risk through putbacks were driving the increase in the price of intermediation, we would expectϕ$\phi$to have increased more on higher-risk loans.38However, we find thatϕ$\phi$evolves similarly if we lower the assumed FICO score from 750 to 680 (see FigureIA.5), even though a FICO-680 loan has higher delinquency risk.
  A related hypothesis is that the positive relationship betweenϕ$\phi$(π$\pi$) and applications, or the positive trend in the series, are due to changes in the expected lifespan of the originated loans. If there is a constant per-period cost of an open loan (e.g., due to the putback risk just discussed), variation in the expected lifespan would translate into differences inπ$\pi$(as originators want to be compensated upfront if expected lifetime costs increase). However, direct proxies for the expected lifespan of newly originated mortgages are not positively related toπ$\pi$(orϕ$\phi$)—seeInternet Appendix Section II.
  Note, however, that an absence of differential changes inπ$\pi$orϕ$\phi$across loans with different credit risk or longer lifespan does not rule out that increased aversion to putbacks was a significant factor driving an upward trend in origination costs, for instance, due to the need for more qualified underwriting personnel and more time spent on processing each loan. Our evidence in SectionV.B.4is consistent with this channel.
  In this subsection, we examine some of the potential explanations for the increase inϕ$\phi$using cross-location differences inϕ$\phi$(or the absence thereof).
  For the October 2009 to October 2014 period, we have access to a separate set of Optimal Blue searches that was run at a weekly frequency across 40 MSAs. We defineϕijt$\phi _{ijt}$as the price of intermediation for lenderi$i$in MSAj$j$at timet$t$. We collapse the data to a monthly frequency to be consistent with both the analysis above and the frequency of the covariates we discuss below.
  First, we study whether there are significant differences in the average price of intermediation across MSAs. To do so, we regressϕijt$\phi _{ijt}$on lender-by-month fixed effects and MSA fixed effects, with Los Angeles—the MSA we have used so far for our analysis—as the omitted MSA. The resulting estimated MSA effects along with 95% confidence intervals are shown in Figure9. Relative to the overall fluctuations inϕ$\phi$, the cross-MSA variation is small (the range is just over 0.1 points). This is in line with the findings of Hurst et al. (2016).
  Nevertheless, we use the cross-MSA sample to test two potential explanations for the increase in the price of intermediation. First, we consider the possibility that increased market concentration led to increases in profits. If this were true, a regression ofϕijt$\phi _{ijt}$on the market share of the top four lenders in MSAj$j$in montht−1$t-1$(similar to Scharfstein and Sunderam2013) would show a positive relationship. Second, if price increases were driven by higher credit risk, we would expect a positive relationship betweenϕijt$\phi _{ijt}$and the delinquency rate of loans originated in MSAj$j$over the two years prior to montht−1$t-1$(similar to Hurst et al.2016).39
  Results of these regressions appear in TableVI. In a first specification, we only add lender fixed effects. Column (1) shows that in this specification, the relationship betweenϕ$\phi$and market concentration is strongly negative, in line with the unconditional relationship seen earlier: whileϕ$\phi$increased over time, market concentration decreased. Next, we add month fixed effects, which means that only cross-sectional variation is used. Column (2) shows no relationship betweenTop 4share andϕ$\phi$. This remains true if we add MSA fixed effects in the most stringent specification (column (3)).
  Turning to the relationship with local delinquency rates, we see that the estimated coefficients arenegative, which is the opposite of the sign one would expect if higher local delinquency risk were priced by lenders. However, the economic magnitude of the estimated effect is very small, also when controlling for both measures of local conditions jointly. In sum, there is little systematic price variation across MSAs at the level we measure it (rate sheets available to LOs), and no evidence that some MSAs are more expensive due to higher market concentration or delinquency risk.
  The evidence presented so far suggests that increases in costs are likely to provide the best explanation for the upward trends inϕ$\phi$andπ$\pi$over 2008 to 2014. To provide some direct evidence on this point, we turn to the Quarterly Mortgage Bankers Performance Report from the MBA. Available since 2008:Q3, this report collects information on origination volumes, costs, and revenues of nonbank mortgage originators.40There are generally between 300 and 350 companies reporting; only aggregate data are released (available for purchase from MBA). The sample composition changes over time, and over our period of interest (2008:Q3 to 2014:Q4) the size of reporting firms increased considerably: the average number of production employees went from about 120 to 300. Similarly, the average number of loans originated in a quarter went from under 1,000 in the 2008 to 2009 period to about 1,650 in 2014, even as marketwide originations overall were shrinking over the same period.41Thus, it is difficult to know how representative of the market these data are at a given point in time, or how comparable they are over time. Nevertheless, they certainly provide a sense of how costs and productivity evolved over our period of interest.
  The report separates Direct Loan Production Expenses into four subcategories: Personnel, Occupancy and Equipment, Technology-Related Expenses, and Other Direct Expenses. Personnel costs account for 65% to 70% of loan production expenses, with the share increasing slightly over time. Expenses are measured in terms of bps per loan dollar.
  A challenge in characterizing the evolution of costs over time is that many of these costs have an important fixed component, so that they are naturally lower when origination volumes are higher. To account for this effect, we regress total expenses, as well as each expense subcategory, on a linear time trend and the average volume of loans originated by the reporting firms. The coefficient on the linear time trend then indicates the average quarterly change in a given expense component over our period of interest.42
  Panel A of TableVIIpresents the results. On average, loan production expenses increased by almost 8.3 bps per quarter. Increases in personnel costs account for about 68% of this total increase, which is almost exactly in line with their average share of total expenses. The other components also all display a significant upward trend, with magnitudes roughly proportional to their averages.43
  For personnel expenses, the MBA report contains an additional five subcategories: sales personnel, fulfillment personnel, production support employees, management and directors, and benefits. We interpret sales personnel as corresponding to the LOs that interact directly with borrowers, while fulfillment and production support correspond to loan underwriting, documentation, and so on. Panel B of TableVIIshows that all personnel cost components increased. Looking across components, the relative increase is stronger for fulfillment and production support than for sales personnel, in line with “back end” costs having risen more, due to more intense underwriting.44
  Panel C of TableVIIpresents direct evidence on the evolution of the productivity of different employee types involved in loan production, measured in terms of the average monthly loan closings per employee. Here, we see a downward trend when considering all employees (column (1)) as well as the three provided subcategories. The trend is more negative for fulfillment and production support employees than sales employees, again suggesting an increase in underwriting effort over time.45This evidence is in line with the finding of an upward trend in loan-processing times over this period, as shown in TableVearlier.
  Finally, increasing fixed costs should disproportionately burden lower-balance loans. InInternet Appendix Section III, we augment our pricing data to include low-balance loans and show that the price of intermediation did indeed rise faster for low-balance loans ($150,000) than for our baseline sample ($300,000). As discussed above, this increase in fixed costs could reflect an increase in putback aversion or the costs of new regulations introduced over this period. For example, to ensure that a loan receives Qualified Mortgage (QM) status, lenders must verify a borrower's “ability to pay,” a task that is not proportional, or even related, to the size of the loan. In addition, agencies like Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac and mortgage insurers tightened their record-keeping and documentation requirements. In fact, our findings here likely understate the increase in fixed costs because evidence suggests that lenders rationed small loans on the extensive margin. That is, rather than charge borrowers more, lenders simply avoided making small loans.46
  Figure10shows the evolution ofϕ$\phi$,π$\pi$, andκ$\kappa$and the differences between them over our sample period. Our measure ofκ$\kappa$uses the data from the MBA analyzed in SectionV.B.4. Specifically, we calculate the net costs of underwriting as all direct costs of producing the loanlessincome received by the intermediary directly from the borrower.47Aκ$\kappa$of zero (as appears early in the sample) does not mean that the loan cost nothing to produce, but rather that direct payments from the borrower covered all of the production costs and therefore any gain-on-sale was income to the intermediary.
  Figure10illustrates why we conclude that the upward trend in the price of intermediation reflects increases in costs and not increased intermediary profits. The figure shows that servicing costs,ϕ−π$\phi -\pi$, account for a significant portion of the growth inϕ$\phi$, as documented in SectionV.A. And direct costs of underwritingκ$\kappa$also show strong growth, as shown in SectionV.B.4. Removing those two cost components yields a profit path without an upward trend, even though at times of high demand (as in early 2009 or in 2012) implied profits are substantial. Note that the reported costs are average costs, not marginal costs. This likely explains why at times theπ−κ$\pi - \kappa$series decreases below zero.48
  In sum, while our measures of (net) cost are certainly not perfect, our evidence suggests that increases in servicing and underwriting costs accounted for the substantial upward trend in the price of intermediation in the years following the financial crisis. The conclusion that mortgage lending did not persistently become more profitable over time is also consistent with the declining number of active lenders over this period; as noted earlier, the number of active lenders as measured in HMDA decreased by over 10% from 2010 to 2014. Furthermore, many large banks reduced their footprint in the (securitized) mortgage market in the years following the financial crisis. This likely also reflects the heightened perceived legal liability risk during this period and the lawsuit exposure in the wake of the crisis (e.g., Buchak et al.,2018; D'Acunto and Rossi,2022).
article.VI. Aggregate Implications:
Over the 73-month period that we cover in the paper, U.S. households refinanced 6.4 trillion dollars of mortgages and used 3.5 trillion dollars of new mortgage debt to purchase homes.49TableVIIIshows that, according to our estimates, households implicitly paid 155 billion dollars to financial intermediaries for their services in these transactions (100.1 billion dollars for refinances and 54.9 billion dollars for purchase mortgages), or about 25 billion per year on average. We arrive at this estimate by using our monthly average estimates ofϕ$\phi$from SectionVand multiplying them by the dollar amount of monthly originations.50Using our different measures of the price of intermediation, we can decompose the spending and find that 131.7 billion dollars or 85% of the spending went to pay for loan origination and 23.3 billion dollars or 15% went to pay for servicing.
  As a point of comparison to assess how large these numbers are, Hurst et al. (2016) find that the GSE policy of maintaining uniform mortgage rates across locations (rather than letting rates vary with credit risk) implied a redistribution across U.S. regions of $14.5 billion (in net PV terms) over the 2007 to 2009 period, or roughly $5 billion per year.
  In SectionV, we document an upward trend in the price of intermediation over our sample period. We can use our estimates from TableIVto ask what would have happened if we set the time trend coefficient to zero. According to our estimates, borrowers would have saved $97 billion, or about 1% of the total origination volume, roughly equally split between origination and servicing expense. Using an average rebate/rate slope of 4.58 (see TableI), this implies that the time trend generated an average 21 bp increase in mortgage interest rates at origination over our sample.
  Another counterfactual question relates to the importance of the sensitivity of the price of intermediation to variation in applications. If we set the coefficient on application volume to zero in our regressions, which is equivalent to assuming that applications were at their average level throughout the sample period, then total spending on intermediation would fall by an additional $11.6 billion. Thus, intermediation prices being high at times of high application volume, which we argue is driven by capacity constraints, has substantial aggregate costs to borrowers as well, although they are not nearly as large as the costs of the upward trend over this period.
  A natural question that arises is whether it is reasonable to useϕ$\phi$in October 2008 as the “reference level” in our counterfactuals given that it is substantially lower than what we observe over the rest of our sample period. While we do not have Optimal Blue data that go back further, a similar calculation based on the Freddie Mac survey rate implies thatϕ$\phi$was roughly constant at this low level over 2006 to 2008 (seeInternet Appendix Section I). Thus, it is not the case that the starting point of ourϕ$\phi$series is “unusual” relative to historical values.
  Importantly, we are not making any welfare statements. What the numbers above reflect is a transfer of the surplus generated by higher MBS prices from households to intermediaries, taking the volume of loan originations as fixed. This is not to say that the high price of intermediation could not have welfare effects. In particular, hadϕ$\phi$remained at its lower original level, this would have led to additional mortgage originations that would have generated surplus for both borrowers and intermediaries.51In contrast, if the increased price of intermediation reflects increased spending on underwriting, higherϕ$\phi$has offsetting benefits, such as fewer mortgage defaults, that must be weighed in a full welfare analysis.
article.VII. Conclusion:
Over the 2000 to 2014 period, residential mortgage originations in the United States averaged about 2.2 trillion dollars per year (Inside Mortgage Finance,2016). Given the size of the market, even relatively small changes in the price of intermediation, defined as the difference between the value of a mortgage's cash flows to investors in the MBS market and what a borrower receives in funding, add up to significant changes in implicit borrower costs. In this paper, we systematically study how the price of intermediation evolved over the 2008 to 2014 period, during which monetary policy and other macroeconomic and financial factors led to record low interest rates.
  We find a significant upward trend in the price of intermediation that appears to be driven by increased net costs of mortgage servicing and the increased labor intensity of mortgage underwriting, potentially due to new regulations and increased liability risk. In addition, there are substantial fluctuations around this trend that are closely related to the level of demand for new mortgages; this finding is indicative of capacity constraints in mortgage originations. We estimate that the upward trend and the sensitivity to demand together raised intermediation costs for borrowers by about $110 billion over the 73 months in our sample, holding origination volumes constant.
  About two-thirds of total household expenditures on intermediation over this period, $100 billion, involved refinances of existing mortgages. One can interpret this figure as one of the costs of having a mortgage market that is dominated by fixed-rate mortgages, since mortgagors need to refinance to benefit from lower rates. Expenditures on intermediation could potentially be much lower if refinancing did not require a full reunderwriting of the mortgage even though the reduction in the monthly payment will generally lead to reduced credit risk relative to the outstanding loan.52Streamlining the process would not only save borrowers time, but potentially also substantial amounts of money. Another way to reduce borrowers' intermediation expenses would be to have more adjustable-rate mortgages, since in that case the transmission of interest rate changes to household cash flow requires no action by financial intermediaries.
  Capacity constraints in mortgage intermediation have affected the transmission of monetary policy. Even though we show that offers to borrowers in the primary mortgage market generally respond very quickly to MBS price changes, we also find that the pass-through of MBS price increases is subdued when application volumes are high. This result implies that policy actions that increase MBS prices—for instance, an announcement of increased QE asset purchases—will pass through less strongly if demand for mortgages is already high. Put differently, in such a situation, intermediaries may absorb a large chunk of the increase in MBS values, rather than pass it on to borrowers. Thus, intermediation frictions are important for policymakers to consider when designing policy actions that primarily target the mortgage market.
